nativeRange.habitat: Rocky reef areas, cave entrances, and crevices along drop-offs, typically at depths of 3–45 m

diet.notes: Tends to prefer live or meaty foods; live saltwater feeder shrimp are commonly recommended when first introducing the fish to the aquarium. Once acclimated, frozen preparations such as mysis shrimp, krill, and silversides have been accepted. Feeding 2–4 times weekly in the evening (or at dusk) is generally advised to accommodate its nocturnal feeding habits.

imageCandidates.notes: George Berninger Jr., 2048x1536. Full lateral view with dorsal, anal and pelvic fins fully fanned out; vivid blue-white spots on dark blue-black body and the dorsal-fin ocellus (false eye) clearly visible. Sharp, high impact showpiece shot.

Diego Delso, 4833x3222. Striking in-situ shot at Anilao, Philippines: full lateral, fins spread, vivid blue spots and yellow-edged dorsal ocellus against a dark reef crevice. Sharp, natural-habitat variety.
Ewen Roberts, 1500x1080. Clean aquarium lateral with electric-blue spots, flowing finnage and dorsal ocellus. Sharp, vivid, correctly identified.

summary: The Marine Betta, also known as the Comet, is a nocturnal reef fish from the Indo-Pacific known for a remarkable mimicry defense — when threatened, it resembles the head of a moray eel. Generally considered intermediate to keep, it tends to hide during daylight and will prey on small fish and ornamental shrimp.
careNotes: Calloplesiops altivelis is a primarily nocturnal species that tends to hide in caves and crevices during daylight hours, becoming more active at dusk. A well-aquascaped tank with caves, overhangs, and live rock is generally considered essential for this species to thrive. When first introduced, live saltwater feeder shrimp are commonly recommended to encourage feeding; frozen meaty foods such as mysis, krill, and silversides are typically accepted once the fish has settled in. The species has a well-documented mimicry defense: when threatened, it pokes its head into a hole and exposes its posterior, which — with its large ocellus (false eye) near the dorsal fin — resembles the head of a moray eel. The Marine Betta is generally reported to be peaceful with fish too large to eat, but should be considered a predator with respect to small fish and ornamental shrimp. Captive-bred specimens are commercially available and may acclimate more readily than wild-caught individuals. Males have been documented guarding egg clutches; females may not display the broader finnage seen in males.
breedingNotes: Breeding has been reported in captivity, with males guarding egg masses attached to rocky surfaces. Captive-bred specimens are commercially available.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (8 in = 20.3 cm), temperature (72–80°F = 22–27°C), lifespan (10 years), care level (difficult). Lifespan averaged with FishBase max age (9 years) to give 8–10 year range.

fish.breedingNotes: Males guard egg clutches on rocky surfaces; captive-bred specimens are commercially available. Sex change has been reported in captive all-male groups, though the mechanism is not fully understood.
